scenario: A FEEL expression must check whether any order in a list has total > 10000 (return true if at least one does). Another need: check whether every order in the list has status = "PAID".
question: Which FEEL syntax fits?

options.text: Built-in functions any(list<boolean>) and all(list<boolean>) paired with a projecting for ... return ... status = "PAID"). The any() function returns true if at least one element in the boolean list is true; all() returns true only if every element is true. Note there is no built-in every() function -- only the every keyword in quantified expressions.

status = "PAID"]) -- a bracket-comprehension syntax. FEEL does not have a bracket-comprehension form using [x in collection : predicate] syntax. The bracket notation on lists in FEEL is for filtering -- list[condition] with the implicit item variable -- not for projection. Additionally, every() is not a FEEL built-in function; every is only a keyword in the quantified expression every x in y satisfies z.

FEEL does not support quantified expressions or any mechanism for checking whether any or all elements in a list satisfy a predicate. This is factually incorrect. FEEL includes both quantified expression syntax (some ... satisfies, every ... satisfies) and equivalent built-in functions (any(), all()) for list-wide predicate evaluation. Both approaches are documented in the FEEL specification and implemented in Camunda 8's Zeebe FEEL engine.

status = "PAID" returns true only if all orders have status PAID. These are native FEEL language keywords -- some and every -- paired with the satisfies keyword. They are the canonical, purpose-built FEEL syntax for existential and universal quantification over lists.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL's quantified expressions are documented at feel-list-expressions.md:88-134: some a in b satisfies c returns true if c holds for one or more elements (:97-98); every a in b satisfies c returns true if c holds for all elements (:119-120). These are language constructs (not function calls), purpose-built for "any element matches" and "all elements match" predicates over typed lists.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL has built-in list functions any(list) (feel-built-in-functions-list.md:252-279) and all(list) (:223-250) which work on boolean lists; pair with a projection via for x in xs return predicate (feel-control-flow.md:35-53) to map non-boolean elements to booleans first. Note: docs explicitly state all() replaced deprecated and(), and any() replaced deprecated or() (:247-249 + :276-278). There is no every() function -- every is only a language keyword in every ... satisfies form.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no bracket-comprehension [a in b : c] form (Pattern #23: 0 hits across docs/components/modeler/feel/). The bracket-on-list form is filter syntax -- [1,2,3,4][even(item)] -- with implicit item variable (feel-list-expressions.md:75-86), not a projection.

d.text: Correct. Both quantified expressions AND built-in functions exist.

explanation: Both quantified expressions AND built-in functions exist.
